---
title: "Simulator and sensitivity internals"
subtitle: "Contributor notes for `crossLagR`"
author: "Authored by Claude (Anthropic); maintainer review by C. Weber"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  eval: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Scope"}
**Internal contributor doc.** Excluded from the built package via
`.Rbuildignore`. Read this on GitHub before changing anything in
`simCLPM`, `simRICLPM`, `simFromSyntax`, `populate_unified_labels`, or
`sensitivityCLPM`. The substantive motivation lives in the [CLPM chapter
of the user guide](https://crweber9874.github.io/crossLagR/book/01_Panel_Models.html);
this is the implementation-level companion.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Authorship"}
Authored by Claude (Anthropic) under the prompt:
> *"now create a simple internal document that describes how to use the
> simulator. I've generally worked out the math correctly, but include a
> detailed (though concise and straightforward) well explained walkthrough
> of why the variance is this, and the characteristics of these new
> sensitivity functions. It can be a quarto in vignettes."*

Then converted to Quarto with formulas elaborated, per follow-up prompt
*"make quarto and include formula."* Maintainer review by C. Weber.
:::

## Two routes to a simulated dataset

There are two ways to manufacture data in `crossLagR`:

1. **Direct simulators** (`simCLPM`, `simRICLPM`, `simCLPMu`) hard-code a
   specific lavaan population model. Each takes substantive arguments
   (`beta_x`, `omega_xy`, `var_p`, `var_BX`, ...) and emits a
   `list(model, data)` where `model` is the lavaan syntax used and `data`
   is the simulated wide-format data frame.
2. **The generic path** (`simFromSyntax` + `populate_unified_labels`)
   takes any estimator-syntax function (e.g. `estimateRICLPM`),
   substitutes user-supplied numeric values into the unified labels
   ($ar\_x$, $cl\_xy$, $d\_var\_x$, ...), and calls
   `lavaan::simulateData()` on the populated syntax.

Use a direct simulator when you need control over a specific DGP
(particularly `simCLPMu`, the only one that injects a time-varying
confounder). Use the generic path when you want to simulate from an
arbitrary unified-label estimator's own implied population.

## Direct simulators

### `simCLPM`

The CLPM population at the latent level is

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_t &= \beta_x\, p_{t-1} + \omega_{xy}\, q_{t-1} + \zeta^p_t, \\
q_t &= \beta_y\, q_{t-1} + \omega_{yx}\, p_{t-1} + \zeta^q_t,
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-clpm-dynamics}

with observed indicators $y_t = p_t$ and $x_t = q_t$ (perfect indicators,
residual variance fixed at zero) and residuals
$\zeta^p_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_p)$,
$\zeta^q_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_q)$, with within-time
covariance $\mathrm{Cov}(\zeta^p_t, \zeta^q_t) = \sigma_{pq}$.

```r
sim <- simCLPM(waves    = 4,
               beta_x   = 0.4, beta_y = 0.4,
               omega_xy = 0.2, omega_yx = 0.2,
               var_x    = 1,   var_y    = 1,
               cov_xy   = 0.3,
               sample_size = 500, seed = 1)
```

Wave-1 variance can be set independently (`var_y1`, `var_x1`,
`cov_xy1`); subsequent waves share the residual variance defined by
`var_x` and `var_y`.

### `simRICLPM`

The RI-CLPM augments the CLPM with a between-person random intercept on
each construct:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{it} &= \mu_y + I_{y,i} + p_{it}, \\
x_{it} &= \mu_x + I_{x,i} + q_{it},
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-riclpm-decomp}

where the trait factors are

$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_{y,i} \\ I_{x,i} \end{bmatrix}
\sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\begin{bmatrix} \mu_{BY} \\ \mu_{BX} \end{bmatrix},
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma^2_{BY} & \sigma_{BXBY} \\
                 \sigma_{BXBY} & \sigma^2_{BX} \end{bmatrix}
\right),
$$

and within-person factors $(p_{it}, q_{it})$ follow the dynamic
equations (@eq-clpm-dynamics). The signature mirrors that
decomposition: `beta_x` / `omega_xy` are within-person dynamics on $p$
and $q$; `var_p` / `var_q` are dynamic residual variances; `var_BX` /
`var_BY` / `cov_BXBY` parameterize the trait factors.

::: {.callout-important title="Identifiability constraints"}
The trait factors are allowed to covary with each other
($\sigma_{BXBY}$) but are constrained to be **uncorrelated with the
wave-1 within-person factors** $p_{i1}, q_{i1}$. This is the standard
RI-CLPM identification (Hamaker, Kuiper & Grasman, 2015) and is what
makes the trait/within decomposition unique.
:::

### `simCLPMu`

Same dynamic equation as `simCLPM`, but each wave includes an additive
shock from a separate AR(1) confounder $u_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_t &= \beta_x\, p_{t-1} + \omega_{xy}\, q_{t-1} + \gamma_p\, u_t, \\
q_t &= \beta_y\, q_{t-1} + \omega_{yx}\, p_{t-1} + \gamma_q\, u_t, \\
u_t &= \rho\, u_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t,
\quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u).
\end{aligned}
$$

Used to construct DGPs for "how badly does estimator $X$ do when there's
an unmodeled confounder" studies. The confounder is technically observed
in `sim$data` (the function returns `u1..uT` columns too, so you *can*
model it). Drop them when simulating realistic data.

## The generic path: `simFromSyntax`

`simFromSyntax(estimator, waves, sample_size, ar_x, ...)` is a thin
two-step pipeline:

```r
fn         <- crossLagR::estimateCLPM       # or any unified estimator
syntax     <- fn(waves = 4)                 # lavaan with labels ar_x*, etc.
populated  <- populate_unified_labels(      # substitute "ar_x*" -> "0.3*"
                syntax, ar_x = 0.3, ar_y = 0.3,
                cl_xy = 0.1, cl_yx = 0.1,
                d_var_x = 0.5, d_var_y = 0.5,
                d_cov_xy = 0)
dat        <- lavaan::simulateData(populated, sample.nobs = 500,
                                   meanstructure = TRUE)
```

`populate_unified_labels` uses word-boundary regex (`\bar_x\*`) so a
hypothetical `ar_xx` parameter is not a partial match. Labels not
supplied are left at lavaan's `simulateData` defaults --- a known
limitation. Higher-level parameters (growth-factor variances for
ALT/LGM/LCMSR, trait variances for TSO, latent FE variances for BB) end
up at lavaan defaults rather than user-controlled. Worth fixing if
anyone ever needs control over them.

## Variance decomposition in `sensitivityCLPM`

The sensitivity function takes a fitted CLPM and asks: *if the true DGP
had the within-person dynamics I just estimated **plus** between-person
trait variance giving ICC $\kappa$, how badly would a CLPM mis-estimate
the AR/CL parameters?*

### Deriving `var_BX = icc / (1 - icc) * d_var_x`

The intraclass correlation is the fraction of total variance
attributable to the trait:

$$
\mathrm{ICC}_x \;\equiv\; \frac{\sigma^2_{BX}}{\sigma^2_{BX} + \sigma^2_{WX}}.
$$ {#eq-icc-def}

Solve for the trait variance given a target ICC $\kappa$ and a fixed
within-person variance $\sigma^2_{WX}$:

$$
\kappa \,(\sigma^2_{BX} + \sigma^2_{WX}) = \sigma^2_{BX}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\sigma^2_{BX} \,(1 - \kappa) = \kappa\, \sigma^2_{WX}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\boxed{\;
\sigma^2_{BX} \;=\; \frac{\kappa}{1 - \kappa}\,\sigma^2_{WX}\,.
\;}
$$ {#eq-varbx-mapping}

In code:

```r
var_BX <- if (icc == 0) 0 else icc / (1 - icc) * d_var_x
var_BY <- if (icc == 0) 0 else icc / (1 - icc) * d_var_y
```

### Why `d_var_x` is a proxy, not the exact within-person variance

The function uses the CLPM's estimated residual variance `d_var_x` for
$\sigma^2_{WX}$. This is an approximation. We should look up the exact.


$$
\sigma^2_{WX,\text{stationary}}
\;=\; \frac{\sigma^2_q}{1 - \beta_y^2}.
$$ {#eq-stationary-ar1}

The implementation deliberately uses $\sigma^2_q$ (i.e. `d_var_x`)
rather than @eq-stationary-ar1 because:

- For modest AR/CL values ($|\beta| \lesssim 0.5$) the inflation factor
  $1 / (1 - \beta^2)$ is within a few percent (e.g., $\beta = 0.4
  \Rightarrow$ factor $1.19$).
- The bias formula it is illustrating
  ($b^{\text{CLPM}}_{xx} \approx \kappa + (1-\kappa)\,b^{\text{true}}_{xx}$;
  see @eq-bias-formula) is itself a univariate approximation.
- Users care about the **qualitative shape** (CLPM diverges from truth
  as ICC grows), not a calibrated bias point estimate.

If you ever need a calibrated sensitivity (e.g. publishing a bias
correction), replace `d_var_x` with the solution to @eq-lyapunov and
document the change.

### The bias formula being illustrated

The chapter's univariate AR(1) bias decomposition is

$$
b^{\text{CLPM}}_{xx}
\;=\;
\mathrm{ICC}_x
\;+\;
(1 - \mathrm{ICC}_x)\,b^{\text{true}}_{xx},
$$ {#eq-bias-formula}

with two notable special cases:

- If $b^{\text{true}}_{xx} = 0$, the CLPM picks up a **spurious AR equal
  to the ICC**.
- If $b^{\text{true}}_{xx} > 0$, the CLPM AR is **inflated**: it equals
  the truth plus an additive contamination $\mathrm{ICC}_x$ scaled by
  $(1 - b^{\text{true}}_{xx})$.

The cross-lag has an analogous decomposition driven by the **trait
covariance** $\sigma_{BXBY}$:

$$
b^{\text{CLPM}}_{yx}
\;=\;
\underbrace{\frac{\sigma_{BXBY}}{\sigma^2_{BY} + \sigma^2_{WY}}}_{\substack{\text{trait-covariance}\\\text{contamination}}}
\;+\;
(1 - \mathrm{ICC}_y)\, b^{\text{true}}_{yx}.
$$ {#eq-cl-bias-formula}

`sensitivityCLPM` currently fixes $\sigma_{BXBY} = 0$, so the CL curves
in the sensitivity plot are flat in expectation; the AR curves rise
with $\kappa$.

### What the function actually does

```r
sensitivityCLPM(fit, icc_grid, n_sims, sample_size, waves)
```

Pseudocode:

```
extract orig = (ar_x, ar_y, cl_xy, cl_yx, d_var_x, d_var_y, d_cov_xy) from fit
for icc in icc_grid:
  var_BX = icc / (1 - icc) * d_var_x       # eq-varbx-mapping
  var_BY = icc / (1 - icc) * d_var_y
  for s in 1..n_sims:
    dat = simRICLPM(
            waves, sample_size,
            beta_x   = orig$ar_x,  beta_y   = orig$ar_y,
            omega_xy = orig$cl_xy, omega_yx = orig$cl_yx,
            var_p    = d_var_x,    var_q    = d_var_y,
            cov_pq   = d_cov_xy,
            var_BX,  var_BY, cov_BXBY = 0
          )
    record CLPM and RI-CLPM AR/CL estimates fit to dat
```

### Why both CLPM and RI-CLPM are refit

The RI-CLPM is the **oracle** in this sensitivity: it knows the true
structure of the DGP, so its estimates should track the within-person
truth (the dashed baseline) as ICC varies. The CLPM is the **target of
the diagnosis**: it omits the trait, so its estimates drift away from
the truth as ICC grows, exactly as @eq-bias-formula predicts. The gap
between the two curves at any ICC is the visible bias.

The "truth" reference line in `plot.crossLagR_sensitivity` is the
original CLPM fit's estimate, treated as the within-person truth by
construction.

### Things to know before changing this

- `cov_BXBY = 0` is fixed. Allowing trait covariance would activate the
  first term in @eq-cl-bias-formula. If you expose this argument, add a
  regression test asserting the CL bias direction matches
  $\mathrm{sign}(\sigma_{BXBY})$.
- `n_sims = 30` is enough to see the *shape* of the sensitivity curve;
  it is not enough to compute a sharp bias point estimate. The print
  method reports the SD per cell so users can judge MC noise.
- Convergence failures are silently turned into `NA` rows. This is fine
  for the plot (the ribbon just gets wider) but downstream users who
  aggregate `sens$estimates` should pass `na.rm = TRUE`.

## Interpreting the sensitivity plot

The plot has four facets ($ar\_x, ar\_y, cl\_xy, cl\_yx$), each with
two curves (CLPM red, RI-CLPM blue) and a dashed horizontal line at the
baseline.

- **RI-CLPM tracks the baseline.** If it does not, either the DGP is so
  extreme that RI-CLPM is also struggling (high ICC, small N), or there
  is a bug in how `simRICLPM` is being parameterized.
- **CLPM curve rises with ICC for AR parameters.** This is the Hamaker
  bias from @eq-bias-formula. At $\kappa = 0$ both curves should
  coincide.
- **CLPM curve for CL parameters is approximately flat** (with
  `cov_BXBY = 0`). @eq-cl-bias-formula attributes CL bias to the trait
  *covariance*, which is zero here. Letting it drift would activate CL
  bias too.
- **Confidence ribbons (10th--90th pct across `n_sims`)** widen at high
  ICC because the fits are harder.

## Caveats users should know

1. **It is a what-if, not a calibration.** The CLPM estimates you pass
   in are already (potentially) biased; treating them as the
   within-person truth means the "truth reference" is itself uncertain.
   To get the actually-implied within-person dynamics, fit an RI-CLPM
   directly with `estimateRICLPM()`.
2. **Use a small grid for exploration, a dense grid for publication.**
   `n_sims = 30` and 4--6 ICC points is enough to see the shape. Bump to
   `n_sims = 200` and 10+ ICC points if this is going in a paper.
3. **High-ICC cells are noisy.** $\kappa > 0.8$ produces RI-CLPMs that
   frequently fail to converge. The function trims `icc_grid` to
   $[0, 1)$ but does not warn at $0.9$; consider lowering by hand.
4. **It assumes both fits use the same syntax wrappers.** If you pass a
   `fit` that was not built from `estimateCLPM` (e.g., your own custom
   lavaan syntax with non-standard labels), parameter extraction
   silently fails and you get `NA` estimates. The function checks for
   this and errors out before simulating.

## Adding a new sensitivity dimension

If you ever want to sweep a second axis (e.g., AR magnitude alongside
ICC), the simplest extension is:

```r
sensitivityCLPMGrid <- function(fit, icc_grid, ar_inflate_grid, n_sims, ...) {
  # for each combination of (icc, inflate):
  #   set orig$ar_x  <- orig$ar_x  * inflate
  #   set orig$ar_y  <- orig$ar_y  * inflate
  #   call the inner simulation loop from sensitivityCLPM
}
```

Keep the existing `sensitivityCLPM` as the one-axis sweep; expose the
grid version as a separate function so the plot method can specialize on
the one-axis case.

## References

- Hamaker, E. L., Kuiper, R. M., & Grasman, R. P. P. P. (2015). A
  critique of the cross-lagged panel model. *Psychological Methods*,
  20(1), 102--116.
- Usami, S., Murayama, K., & Hamaker, E. L. (2019). A unified framework
  of longitudinal models to examine reciprocal relations.
  *Psychological Methods*, 24(5), 637--657.
- CLPM bias derivation: see the [CLPM chapter](https://crweber9874.github.io/crossLagR/book/01_Panel_Models.html)
  of the `crossLagR` book (Sec. on bias under stable trait
  components).
